blurb: A woody-based evergreen mat smothered in crisp white flower clusters each spring, then holding its glossy dark foliage through the whole Front Range winter.
water: Low to moderate; very drought-tolerant once established in sharp-draining soil
seasons: Dense white flower clusters Apr–May; glossy dark evergreen foliage year-round; trim after bloom for compactness
wildlife: Early-season nectar source for small bees and butterflies; not a primary wildlife plant
deer: Moderately deer-resistant; not a top choice for deer
toxic: Mildly toxic — all parts contain glucosinolates; keep away from pets and children who might ingest

care.hardiness: USDA zones 3–8; fully cold-hardy on the Front Range (5b–6a) and holds its evergreen foliage through Boulder winters without protection. The woody base is more cold-hardy than the soft tips — a late hard frost after bud break can brown shoot tips, but the plant recovers.
care.planting: Plant in-ground in spring (late April–May, after the last hard frost) or in early fall (September) so roots establish before freeze-up. Fall planting is ideal for Front Range clay soils — cooler temps and autumn moisture let roots settle without summer heat stress. In containers, plant in spring; candytuft in pots dries out faster than in-ground and the roots are less cold-hardy — water more frequently in summer heat and overwinter pots in an unheated garage or sink them into the ground to protect the root zone from freeze-thaw cycles.
care.sun: Full sun gives the most prolific bloom and tightest, most compact growth — aim for 6+ hours. It tolerates part shade (3–4 hours) but blooms less freely and can grow looser and more open. On the Front Range, intense afternoon sun is fine if drainage is sharp and the plant is not waterlogged.
care.soil: Sharp drainage is non-negotiable — candytuft will rot in wet clay. Amend heavy Front Range clay with coarse grit or gravel, or plant in a raised bed or slope. Neutral to slightly alkaline pH (6.5–7.5) suits our native soils well; no acidifying amendments needed. Lean soil is fine; rich, high-nitrogen soil promotes foliage at the expense of flower.
care.water: Low once established — water deeply every 1–2 weeks in summer, allowing soil to dry between waterings. It is very drought-tolerant in well-drained soil. Overwatering or standing moisture, especially in winter, is the most common cause of death. In Front Range clay, water sparingly in winter even if the plant looks dry.
care.spacing: Space plants 12–18 in apart for a ground-cover effect; they spread slowly by rooting stems and will fill in over 2–3 seasons. For edging or rock walls, 12 in spacing gives a solid ribbon within two years.
care.propagation: Seed can be sown indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost at 65–70°F; germination is slow and variable (2–4 weeks) and seedlings of named cultivars may not come true. The easiest reliable method is stem cuttings: take 3–4 in softwood cuttings in early summer (June) just after bloom, strip the lower leaves, dip in rooting hormone, and stick in moist perlite or coarse sand; roots form in 3–4 weeks under humidity. Layering (pegging a trailing stem to the soil until it roots) also works. Division of established clumps is difficult due to the woody crown — cuttings are strongly preferred.
care.bloom: April into May on the Front Range, sometimes extending into early June at elevation or in cool springs. The display lasts 3–4 weeks — a carpet of flat white corymbs. A light second flush sometimes appears in fall if plants are sheared back in summer.
care.feeding: Little or no fertilizer needed. A light top-dress of balanced slow-release granules (10-10-10) in early spring is sufficient if the soil is very poor. Avoid high-nitrogen feeds, which push lush growth prone to flopping and fungal issues in our semi-arid climate.
care.maintenance: Shear plants back by one-third immediately after bloom (late May–early June) — this is the single most important task. Hard shearing keeps the mat tight, prevents woody legginess, and often prompts a modest second flush of growth. Without shearing, plants become open and woody in the center within a few years. Remove dead stems in spring once new growth is visible.
care.troubles: Root rot in poorly drained or overwatered soil is the main killer — ensure sharp drainage. Clubroot (a soilborne pathogen of the Brassica family) can occur in heavy, wet soils; rotate planting sites if it appears. Occasional attack by imported cabbageworm caterpillars (also a Brassica relative); hand-pick or treat with Bt if needed. Generally trouble-free in Front Range conditions with good drainage.
